title: Coverage of essential health services
subtitle: Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.
caption:
Coverage of essential health services, 2023

Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.

The choropleth map displays the 2023 coverage of essential health services across the globe, utilizing a color gradient where light green represents the lowest scores near 0 and deep blue represents the highest scores approaching 100. The highest values appear across western and central Europe, including France, Germany, and the Nordic countries, as well as in Australia, Japan, and the United States, where the color falls in the darkest blue band indicating scores between 80 and 100. Canada and most of South America, including Brazil and Argentina, also show high coverage in the 60 to 80 range. In contrast, the lowest values are visible across most of sub-Saharan Africa, where countries like Chad, the Central African Republic, and Somalia sit in the lightest green band between 0 and 20. Parts of South Asia, including Afghanistan and Pakistan, along with Yemen in the Middle East, also display low coverage in the 20 to 40 range. India and China occupy the middle of the distribution, with colors corresponding to the 40 to 60 band. Greenland and a few other territories are marked with hatching to indicate no data is available. The geographic distribution shows a clear divide, with the highest index scores concentrated in high-income regions of the Northern Hemisphere and Oceania, while the lowest scores cluster in lower-income regions of Africa and parts of Asia.
